data.title: IDEXX Laboratories: I Love The Business, Not The Stock Price
data.description:
Introduction

IDEXX's (NASDAQ:IDXX) stated vision for the company is,

To be a great company that creates exceptional long-term value for our customers, employees, and shareholders by enhancing the health and well-being of pets, people, and livestock.

The company aims to achieve this vision through six avenues, as shown below.

The Company's Revenue Drivers

IDXX reportable revenue has three segments, the primary one is CAG or Companion Animal Group, "a reporting segment that provides veterinarians diagnostic products and services and information management solutions that enhance the health and well-being of pets" (2024 10K).

Other than CAG, IDXX also reports revenue from Water quality products, and Livestock, Poultry and Dairy (LPD). The Other operating segment is very small, making up only 0.4% of IDXX's total revenue, so I will not be discussing that.

Five Reasons Why I Like The Business

One: Strong Recurring Revenue

I love a business with recurring revenue.

See the second-largest segment below, called "Consumables"?

Consumables make up 36% of IDXX's CAG sales in 2024, up from 35% in 2023, a segment that boasts the second-largest growth rate of 9.7%. Strictly speaking, consumables are not recurring revenue in the subscription-model sense of the word, i.e. customers are not contractually obligated to buy these consumables. IDXX does not include "consumables" under recurring revenue (more on that later). However, once IDXX's clients buy IDXX's analyzers, they have no choice but to purchase IDXX proprietary consumables frequently and predictably. And that is why I consider this stream of income as "recurring".

Let's illustrate this with one example, IDXX's Catalyst One Chemistry Analyzer, a clinic's on-site laboratory for dogs, cats, exotic animals, horses and livestock.

(This link brings you to the official operation manual, while this link brings you to a video explanation. The description below is a simplified, reconstructed version based on the video and the manual.)

Say a vet wants to find do a routine health check on a pet dog's kidney function. The vet will need a blood sample from the animal. Collecting the blood requires a syringe, a needle, and a lithium heparinized blood separator. Next, a chemistry CLIP (with preloaded set of catalyst slides) is needed to be inserted into the Catalyst One together with the blood. After that, a SDMA slide (Symmetric Dimethylarginine) and SDMA reagent are loaded into the Catalyst One. All the vet needs to do now is to press the start button, and 10 min to 15 min is all it takes for the Catalyst One analyzer to process the blood sample and display the results on the analyzer, and then subsequently transfer that data into the IDEXX VetLab Station, a touch screen, centralized hub that comes bundled with the Catalyst One (and other IDEXX analyzers) used for managing and organizing diagnostic data across the different IDEXX analyzers. On a monthly basis, the vet will conduct a calibration of Catalyst One, and to perform that the vet will need to procure IDXX's Catalyst SmartQC Solution.

All the consumables mentioned above (syringe, needle, lithium heparinized blood separator, SDMA slide and reagent, SmartQC Solution) are not meant to be reused, so new ones have to be purchased from IDXX for the duration of use for Catalyst One.

And IDXX sells other analyzers, not just Catalyst One, each with its set of proprietary consumables, so once a consumer buys an analyzer, the company benefits from recurring revenues from consumables.

How would the data be collected across these different analyzers? Regardless of the analyzers used, the data is consolidated across different IDXX's analyzers for each patient, making the documenting and referencing of patient records easy. This streamlined process makes it easy for the vet to collect data electronically rather than manually, reducing errors.

Then, there is the smaller segment called "recurring revenue", under Veterinary software, services and diagnostic imaging systems. This is the software part. There are two software options for customers to choose from. There is ezyVet, a cloud-based practice management software that improves team productivity and streamlines patient care. There is also Neo Software, a cloud-based software with an intuitive dashboard to help users manage daily activity, manage appointments, get a clear overview of overdue invoices, reminders due and draft invoices, has a one-click feature to access complete patient history, and much more.

Although this portion of the total revenue is not the largest, just 7% of the CAG's revenue segment, but its growth is the highest at 16.7%. Without this recurring revenue portion, CAG's total revenue growth will fall from 6.6% to 5.9%.

Over time, as the number of users expands, the software segment will also grow and will become a significant contributor to IDXX's overall revenue and profit (profit margin is the highest for software), which can pull up the overall results from any lag in sales growth of the hardware.

Two: Sticky Business Model

I love business with a sticky business model.

As described in the Catalyst One example above, it is easy to imagine that once IDXX's customers start to use the products, software, training services, build up their contacts with their clients, and consolidated the patients' medical history in one easy-to-access screen, it can be difficult, costly, and time-consuming to switch to a different service provider. It is sticky.

This is why CEO Jay Mazelsky was confident in declaring at Q4 2024 earnings call,

Our partnership approach has yielded exceptional customer retention rates of over 97%, ensuring that practices continue to see IDEXX as an indispensable partner in their growth and efficiency goals. Customers trust IDEXX to help them navigate challenges of staffing and visit trends.

IDXX's sales team works well with the customers, providing them with excellent support, and is able to sell to new customers and upsell to many existing customers. CEO Mazelsky continued,

IDEXX commercial teams continue to operate at a high-level this year, supporting new business gains, solid net price realization, high-90%s customer retention levels across major testing modalities, and sustained adoption levels of diagnostic testing utilization near post-pandemic highs. High placement levels across our core premium instrument platforms drove a 9% growth in our premium instrument installed base.

The following note from Morningstar analyst Debbie S. Wang is illuminating,

Beyond these technology-based intangibles, Idexx’s major secret weapon lies in its sales representatives and field organization that provide high-touch service to animal hospitals. Idexx reps have proven themselves successful in providing counsel on optimizing hospital operations and improving the profit profile for vets. We have yet to see any other animal health firms carve out this sort of integrated role in animal hospital operations.

Three: Strong Tailwinds

I love business that operates in an industry bolstered by strong tailwinds.

According to data from CapitalOneShopping, the US pet industry revenue has been growing steadily, more than doubled since 2016.

Pet owners are willing to spend on their pets, and the total vet-related expenditure (surgical vet + routine vet visit) comes to a huge 42%.

Four: Predictable Revenue

I love business with predictable and growing revenue.

Over the past twenty years, IDXX managed to grow revenue every year, through the Great Financial Crisis, the crash in 2018, COVID-19, and the bear market from 2022 to 2023. FactSet analysts are projecting continual growth moving forward, and with 100% accuracy rate for their 1-year and 2-year sales forecast, this is as predictable as one can get.

And revenue is not just growing in the United States but internationally as well. CEO Mazelsky shared at the Q4 2024 earnings call that,

Our international regions showcased strong performance in 2024, contributing significantly to our growth story. Double-digit expansion of our premium instrument installed base outside the US underscored the benefits of our targeted global commercial investments over the past three years. Our maturing international sales organization has not only secured high-quality placements but also driven consistent diagnostic utilization gains, particularly in the Europe and Asia-Pacific regions, where we continue to see untapped potential. Spotlighting our European region's very strong performance, we saw the seventh consecutive quarter of double-digit CAG Diagnostics recurring revenue growth, with IDEXX VetLab consumables growth in the high-teens.

Five: Innovation Led Growth Strategy

IDXX spends an increasing amount on R&D every year.

The amount spent in the last five years ($968 million) increased by 72% to the amount spent in the previous five years ($561 million). That helped the company come up with new products and grew the revenue by 59%, from $9.966 billion in 2015 to $$16.849 billion in 2024.

One example of the latest product is the IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer that provides "automated, real-time cytology and blood morphology provide results with reference laboratory-level accuracy, within 10 minutes", a new tool that saves vets precious time. CEO Mazelsky says this tool is well received,

The launch of IDEXX inVue Dx has been one of the most exciting developments in 2024, marking a new era in Point-of-Care diagnostics...

Customer interest for IDEXX inVue Dx continues to be exceptionally strong, with nearly 1,600 preorders globally by the end of Q4, reflecting the high demand for this next-generation platform. International interest has been similarly robust as we began taking orders in select geographies in Q4...

... we initiated a controlled launch of IDEXX inVue Dx in Q4 and, to ensure a best-in-class customer experience, we will gradually ramp up placements through 2025...

Customers are simply amazed with how easy the analyzer is to use, eliminating the need for complex slide preparation or interpretation, with results in minutes.

Underpinning the financial guidance Andrew shared is a placement target for 4,500-plus inVue placements over the course of the year, as we march towards our 20,000 placement goal over five years.

Four Risks To Keep Tabs On

One: Declining Growth Rate

Although the average growth rate for the past twenty years was an impressive 13.33%, that declined to 8.03% for the past three years. The forward growth for FY 2025 is expected to be even lower, at just 6.36%.

FactSet analysts have repeatedly lowered their sales forecast for IDXX over the past 6 months for FY 2025, 2026 and 2027. CFO Brian McKeon explained the declining growth rate,

We continue to work through pressure on US same-store clinical visit levels, which declined nearly 3% in Q4 and 2% for the full year in 2024. This has been the primary constraint on IDEXX's recent growth, reflecting transitional sector macro impacts following the accelerated expansion of pet healthcare during the pandemic.

Basically, IDXX has tough comps after the blistering 14% and 19% growth rate for 2020 and 2021 respectively pulled forward a lot of sales. Another reason would be macro headwinds, such as fears of a global economic downturn or recession. The effects of tariffs increasing the cost of goods will be touched on next.

Two: China Risk

Senior VP Andrew Emerson acknowledged negative impacts from tariffs,

We're planning for solid gross margin gains on a comparable basis in 2025, supported by growth in CAG Diagnostics recurring revenues, benefits from lab productivity initiatives, and expansion of our high-margin cloud-based software business. This is partially offset by unfavorable business mix planned from higher levels of CAG Diagnostics instrument revenue gains and preliminary estimates for tariff risk on internationally-sourced materials.

The earnings call was held on February 2, 2025, so these estimates could not have factored in the chain of events that happened since then.

You can read more about these events in my previous article (link here).

The China risk is real, but it is minimal. Not only is the revenue segment from China small to begin with, just 0.92% in 2024, that small amount represents a 27% decline from the 1.46% in 2022.

What is unknown now is the cost impact of the materials sourced from China. Due to the stickiness of the business model, it is likely that IDXX can pass the increased costs to its existing customers. It will be harder to make the case for new potential customers, to convince them to switch to IDXX's analyzers in these uncertain times. It will also be harder to upsell and cross-sell to existing customers. Management warns of this in the 2024 10K,

... additional tariffs on imported goods may increase our costs to purchase products, components, and materials. We may not be able to offset these higher costs through productivity initiatives and price increases, which may materially and adversely affect our business, results of operations, and financial condition. Any price increases we may impose may lead to declines in sales volume or loss of business...

Three: Efficiency Risks

Although revenue has been growing steadily over the years, and management has shown itself astute in its acquisitions (e.g. ezyVet is already part of the product line up) without burgeoning the debt load (long-term-debt-to-capital ratio is only at 21%; gross leverage of about 0.7x at the end of 2024 which is down from 1.3x at the end of FY 2022), there are areas of concern.

I will highlight a few.

First, the cash conversion cycle has increased from 82 days in 2020 to 108 days in 2024, signalling customers have been taking longer time to pay. This slows down the conversion of cash from sales.

Secondly, the days inventory outstanding increased from 65 days in 2020 to 91 days in 2024, indicating a slowdown in sales.

Thirdly, both ROE (return on equity) and ROIC (return on invested capital) have been on the decline since 2024. The deleveraging over the years largely caused the ROE to fall. I am less concerned with this, as I feel more assured when a company is more financially sound.

I am more concerned about the decreasing ROIC figures, as it shows how effectively a company generates profit from its total invested capital, and this is something I will keep an eye on. To be clear, IDXX's ROIC is way higher than some of its peers like Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) (8.14%) and ResMed (RMD) (20.87%).

Four: Capital Allocation Risk

Management reduced shares outstanding by 13% from 93 million shares to 82 million shares between 2014 and 2024. Furthermore, management stated plans to purchase another $1.5 billion worth of shares in 2025.

Management’s continued emphasis on share buybacks may suggest a lack of opportunities to redeploy the cash internally or through M&A. While I like shareholder friendly actions like share buybacks and I agree that can be an effective way to return excess capital opportunistically when shares are undervalued.

At current valuation levels, management can consider a more conservative way to allocate capital, taking a page from Warren Buffett's playbook to build a cash reserve in short-term Treasuries, or even start a small dividend. These alternatives to share buybacks could provide management with greater long-term flexibility while signaling to shareholders their confidence.

Valuation

IDXX did well in 2024. Earnings per share increased 6% to $10.67. The company's 2025 earnings per share growth outlook is $11.74 per share to $12.24 per share, an increase of 8% to 12%.

Management's initial full-year earnings per share guidance of $11.74 - $12.24 reflects confidence for solid organic revenue growth. Again, note that the guidance was given on February 3, 2025, before Liberation Day.

FactSet analysts have been lowering their adjusted operating earnings over the past six months. Considering the effects of tariffs, it is fair to lower the projections further.

IDXX has an excellent record of besting analysts' 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (36%), and meeting their 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (64%), showing the reliability of IDXX's ability to generate consistent and predictable earnings.

Noting that IDXX's adjusted operating earning suffered during recessionary periods as well in the form of lower growth (6% growth in 2009), and with the increasing possibility of recession in 2025 and US GDP slowing down, I adjusted bear-case scenario growth rate down to just 3% in 2025, followed by 4% in 2026 and 2027, increased the possibility of my bear-case scenario happening to 30%. I derived at a $397.80 fair value for IDXX, ranging from $249.71 (36% downside) at my low range to $545.43 (39% upside) in my bull scenario. At the current price of $390.94, I do not see a margin of safety nor a favorable risk-reward-ratio. I believe I have been generous in my valuation as I continued to assign a high multiple (ranging from 20 to 30) for a company that is projected to grow at an average low double-digits growth rate for the next few years, deciding that the quality of the company deserves this premium even in the face of potential recession.

Conclusion

I love many things about IDEXX Laboratories. It is a business that is supported by strong tailwinds, has a sticky business model with predictable, recurring revenue, all supported by an innovation-led growth strategy. New products like IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer will be supporting new growth both in the US as well as in international markets from 2025.

At the same time, there are some concerns that as an investor, I would want to be cognizant of, like the way management is allocating capital, the tariff risks involving China, and a declining growth rate.

My main concern will be the current valuation, and as someone who would consider an investment only when I see at least a 10% margin of safety, I have to reluctantly put this on my watchlist.

data.title: MSTY: Payouts Continue To Decline As Bitcoin Cycle Shifts
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY) is arguably one of the most successful high yielding option funds that has been launched in the last year. MSTY currently offers investors a massive distribution yield of 82.8%, based on the most recently paid distribution. The actual dividend yield changes every month as the payout amounts are declared but such a large yield like this provides a ton of utility for investors seeking a high level of income. Unlike most other YieldMax funds, MSTY hasn't fallen significantly below its inception price of approximately $20 per share. However, MSTY is a bit of a unique fund compared to other option ETFs and I believe there may be an elevated risk profile right now due to the fund's indirect association with Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that MSTY's price has fallen by over 48.5%. When actually including all of the distributions that were paid out to shareholders, MSTY's total return jumps up above 48.5% over the same period. MSTY's strategy aims to mimic the daily price movements of common shares of Strategy (MSTR). In order to achieve this, MSTY implements synthetic options that can replicate price movement, but the tradeoff is that there's a capped upside potential.

MSTY's price has generally followed the momentum of Bitcoin, and I am worried about the shift in the crypto cycle. While MSTY provided superior returns when BTC crossed that $100K milestone, the outlook over the next twelve months may not be as attractive. If the price of BTC comes crashing down, it's safe to assume that MSTY will follow that same momentum. So let's start by taking a look at MSTY's underlying investment strategy and compare how the fund has performed against YieldMax peers.

Fund Strategy & Performance Comparison

MSTY has an inception dating back to February 2024, making it only one-year old. Despite the high yield offered, MSTY has a very reasonable gross expense ratio of 0.99%. In order to generate the necessary income that it needs to fund the distributions, MSTY deploys a synthetic option strategy. The term 'synthetic' refers to the fact that MSTY doesn't actually own any common shares of the Strategy. This can elevate the risk profile and cause MSTY's price to experience a higher level of volatility compared to MSTR. MSTY replicates the daily price movements by simultaneously buying call options and selling MSTR put options. The prospectus lays out the different techniques that the fund managers use to generate income.

The strategy is effective because MSTR is such a high volatile stock. For instance, the implied volatility currently sits at 97%. The higher the volatility of a holding, the higher the income premium that can be collected from options. The volatility is a key piece of the equation to why MSTY can continue generating such high amounts of income for shareholders.

MSTY chooses to implement call options that are ATM (at-the-money), which involves selecting a strike price that is at or near the current market price of MSTR. This allows MSTY to generate a sizable amount of premium income from the transaction but doesn't allow for much capital appreciation to be experienced. Plenty of other YieldMax funds using this strategy have already demonstrated that this strategy will usually lead to capital erosion over time.

However, MSTY does utilize an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy with the sold call options, which can allow the fund to partake in price growth during bull markets. An OTM option approach is different because the strike price can be anywhere between 5% to 15% above the current market price of the underlying common stock it aims to track. For instance, MSTY was able to increase over 46% between September and December 2024. While it's great that MSTY was able to partake in some capital appreciation, we can see just how limited the price growth is compared to the underlying shares of MSTR.

The fact that MSTR rose by nearly 200% over a few months is the main contributor to MSTY's exceptional performance. This is likely the reason why MSTY has done a lot better than the majority of other YieldMax funds. For instance, we can see how MSTY's price decline since inception is a lot less dramatic compared to some of the peers. I recently published an article reviewing 4 separate YieldMax funds if you are interested in learning about alternative YieldMax products.

  • YieldMax COIN Option Income Strategy ETF (CONY)
  • YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY)
  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY)
  • YieldMax META Option Income Strategy ETF (FBY)
  • YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY)

These funds have a clear weakness to maintaining the health of its NAV. Any fund that erodes investor capital over the course of 12 months to this extent definitely warrants caution. Therefore, the best-case scenario would be for an investor to hope that a fund is able to pay distributions until the initial amount of invested capital is received back in distributions. This would effectively grant an investor 'house money' status. For instance, MSTY's current dividend yield is a little over 82%. Assuming that distributions can remain at this annualized rate, an investor should be able to receive their initial capital back in distributions in a little over a year. For example:

  • $10,000 invested into MSTY
  • Annualized yield of 82%.
  • Expected to generate ~$8,200 in dividend income over twelve months.
  • 100% of your $10,000 investment would be repaid in approximately 15 months.
  • Once you receive your initial principal back, you have now achieved house money status.

Vulnerability: BTC Decline

MSTR essentially operates as a Bitcoin treasury company. The company now owns more than 528K Bitcoin, with a most recent purchase of 20,356 in a single transaction costing more than $32B. Since the company holds so much Bitcoin as part of its balance sheet, MSTR's price has followed a similar momentum to the price of BTC. Looking at the chart below, we can see that MSTR and BTC share very similar peaks and valleys over the last year. However, MSTY's option strategy doesn't allow it to partake in such drastic upsides over the same period.

Any period where MSTR's price trended downward, ultimately led to MSTY's price falling even further. I worry that when the Bitcoin cycle shifts, MSTR and MSTY's price can collapse and take the fund to all-time lows. Bitcoin historically goes through four-year cycles that are centered around halving events, which shrinks the available reward that can be collected for Bitcoin miners. The last halving even took place in early 2024 and was one of the catalysts that pushed the price of Bitcoin above $100K for the first time.

The typical pattern is for the price of Bitcoin to reach its highest between a twelve to eighteen-month period following a halving event. The last halving event happened on April 20th of 2024, so we are now approaching the twelve-month mark. While the timing around the momentum shifts isn't down to an exact science, I do remain cautious of a downward shift. Entry into MSTY at the current prices could lead to major losses if crypto markets experience this negative shift.

Just as an example, a halving event happened in May 2020. Following this, the price of BTC increased from roughly $9K and had a huge run up above the $60K mark by April 2021. By November 2022, the price of BTC deteriorated back down to about $16K, happening over the span of about a year and a half. Assuming a similar pattern, there's a possibility that the price of BTC sees a crash downwards by the end of 2025. I'd recommend looking at the historical chart that clearly lays out the price movements after each halving event.

Bitcoin peaked around January of this year and slowly deteriorated since then. On a YTD basis, BTC has declined by more than 14%, while MSTY experienced a much more severe downside of 25%, despite MSTR being a bit more resilient than BTC.

Declining Dividends

The most recently declared distribution amounted to $1.3356 per share and is the lowest payout that has been announced since the fund's inception. We can see that there has been a clear downtrend since the highest payout that was issued back in November 2024. The downtrend in payout amounts has led me to question the sustainability of the distributions over the long term.

If we transition into a crypto winter and the price of Bitcoin remains heavily suppressed, MSTY's distributions will likely remain lower until the price of Bitcoin increases. Despite markets being highly volatile, payouts have continued to decline across the board for many other YieldMax funds.

Another example of declining dividend payouts can be seen with CONY as well. CONY shares a very similar weakness to the momentum of Bitcoin, since Coinbase (COIN) is one of the largest public holders of Bitcoin as well.

Even if we look at a fund that doesn't have an underlying link to Bitcoin, such as YieldMax MSFT Option Income Strategy ETF (MSFO), we can see that the payouts continue to decline as markets head lower.

However, there's no denying that the distributions from MSTY have still resulted in significant outperformance in comparison to traditional index-tracking ETFs like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). So while the risk profile may be elevated, so is the reward potential. Despite the declining dividends, MSTY has returned over 48.5% over the last twelve months. While investors would have seen better returns by simply buying shares of the underlying stock of MSTR, a fund like this is great for investors chasing income.

An issue that I have with MSTY is that the reporting documents aren't regularly updated. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice is only dated as of October 2024. This document indicated that half of all distributions were funded by net investment income, while the other half was funded by return of capital. Distributions funded by net investment income are classified as ordinary income, which have the least favorable tax consequences.

However, return of capital distributions aren't actually taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are essentially deferred until the time of sale. Based on your specific situation, a position in MSTY may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate MSTY as a hold at this time due to the underlying risk/reward trade off. On one front, the distributions can be highly rewarding for long-term holders, assuming that the fund is able to maintain a similar annualized distribution rate. Conversely, I am cautious about a shift in the crypto environment following the halving. If the price of Bitcoin follows prior patterns, MSTY's price can continue to slide to all-time lows.

data.title: Sinclair: Broadcaster Poised To Benefit From FCC Deregulation Catalyst
data.description:
Last month, prior to the recent tariff-driven market tumult, investors were clearly starting to catch onto a possible long-term secular trend in the television broadcasting space. Shares in broadcasters, Sinclair (NASDAQ:SBGI) among them, briefly surged during this time frame, as the market began to agree with a bullish thesis emerging for this "old media" industry.

Although the latest macro developments, especially how they stand to play a role in a possible future recession, may highlight near-term risks at hand. Even so, with SBGI stock, not to mention shares in other names sharing the same potential tailwinds, now may be the time to begin accumulating a position, if you've yet to do so.

The trend, of course, is the ongoing efforts by current Federal Communications Commission Chairman Brendan Carr to further deregulate the industry, as well as to possibly open up the door for yet another spectrum auction, which like in the 2010s created a large windfall for broadcast station owners.

Sinclair Stock: Background

Since the 1950s, the Smith family has been involved in the broadcast industry. During the 1970s and 1980s, the Smiths, through Sinclair's corporate predecessor Chesapeake Television, established independent UHF television stations in Baltimore (WBFF) and Pittsburgh (WPNT).

These independent stations benefited from the establishment of the Fox (FOX) television broadcasting network in 1986, when they became charter affiliates in their respective markets. Having changed its name to Sinclair Broadcast Group in 1985, it was in the 1990s that the former Chesapeake Television began its journey to become one of America's largest television station operators.

As, at the time, FCC rules on television station ownership were far more stringent (no duopolies allowed, lower national ownership cap), Sinclair engaged in numerous workarounds, such as local market agreements (LMAs) with third-party station owners, including arrangements where a Smith-controlled shell company would acquire a station, then enter an LMA with a Sinclair-owned station in the same market.

These creative strategies enabled Sinclair to both operate multiple stations in existing markets (benefiting from economies of scale), as well as to expand into other markets (mostly mid-sized metropolitan areas). These stations, nearly all UHF stations either affiliated with Fox or still operating as independents, also benefited from the further growth in popularity of Fox Broadcasting and/or from the launch of other new national TV networks like UPN and the WB.

In the late 90s/early 2000s, Sinclair benefited from the initial relaxation of broadcast ownership rules, including the FCC's first allowance of outright ownership of multiple stations in the same market (duopolies). Sinclair capitalized on this, while at the same time still engaging in workaround efforts, to fully benefit from economies of scale.

M&A activity simmered down during the mid-2000s, but by the early 2010s, Sinclair was once again back in acquisition-mode. This time, the company focused on purchasing station groups, particularly those that owned major network affiliates. A good example of this is Sinclair's 2013 purchase of Allbritton Communications for nearly $1 billion.

To stay in compliance with FCC national station cap and duopoly rules (which still limited the ability to own multiple top-four affiliates in a market), Sinclair sold existing stations to third parties, entering shared-services agreements (SSAs) with them to maintain control/benefit from economies of scale.

During this era, Sinclair also capitalized on the rise of digital broadcast television, and its enabling of digital subchannels. Sinclair started to partner with content library owners like MGM, to create "diginets" that ran re-runs of classic shows and/or niche programming 24/7, generating ancillary revenue for stations.

Sinclair received some regulatory pushback during the first leg of its 2010s acquisition wave, but with the FCC of the first Trump administration accelerating the rolling back of ownership limitation, the company continued to bulk up in size.

These deals were funded in part by the $313 million in gross proceeds generated from the first broadcast spectrum auction, as well as from cash flows bolstered by election-year ad spending and retransmission consent fees from cable operators.

The company even tried to buy station group Tribune, but due to various factors, including what remained of broadcast ownership limits, that deal was eventually scrapped, leading the way for Tribune to eventually be acquired by Nexstar Media Group (NXST), Sinclair's larger competitor.

Alongside further station purchases, Sinclair diversified into cable television network ownerships, most notably with its participation in the 2019 acquisition of regional sports networks from Disney (DIS), through an entity known as Diamond Sports Group.

Yet while this $9.6 billion asset purchase was initially lauded by the market, the Diamond Sports deal would kick off the start of a multi-year slump for SBGI stock.

Recent Developments

Over the past six years, Sinclair shares have experienced a severe drop in price, with most of the declines occurring in the aftermath of the Diamond Sports deal.

This was due to concerns related to Sinclair's high-leveraged balance sheet, as well as disappointment with the Diamond Sports transaction. These factors lead to both analysts and investors tuning out, causing a capitulation made worse by the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic.

Heavy ad spending during the 2020 election cycle may have won back enthusiasm for SBGI stock from investors immediately after this sharp price decline, but the situation with Diamond Sports worsened. Diamond eventually entered Chapter 11 bankruptcy, wiping out SBGI's equity interest.

Alongside the Diamond fiasco, SBGI stock has been weighed down by continued debt-related concerns, namely Sinclair's ability to refinance debt, as the pivot in media consumption from broadcast/cable television to streaming stand to threaten future profitability.

However, recent developments paint a more promising vision for the future. As seen in Sinclair's latest quarterly earnings release, the company performed strongly during Q4 2024. Total revenue came in at around $1 billion, up 22% from the prior year's quarter. Adjusted EBITDA increased 83%, from $180 million to $330 million, and EPS came in at $2.61, a big swing from the $5.35 per share loss reported during Q4 2023.

Yes, heavy ad spending during the 2024 U.S. Presidential campaign was a big factor in this strong year-over-year performance. However, these results topped prior management guidance. Moreover, while revenue fell slightly short of forecasts, normalized earnings beat sell-side guidance by 83 cents per share.

Alongside strong results, Sinclair also provided guidance for the March quarter. During Q1 2025, Sinclair anticipates total revenue of between $765 million and $779 million, and adjusted EBITDA in the $90 million to $102 million range. This guidance clearly underscores how Sinclair's earnings will fall this year, as it's an off-year for U.S. elections, but this figure is in line with the sell-side's forecasts for 2025.

That's not all. As discussed in the earnings release, as well as an Investor Presentation provided alongside the results, there are other promising developments taking shape.

For instance, as discussed in the Investor Presentation, factors like "the Great Rebundling," or cable operators providing cable/streaming subscription packages cheaper than that of tech competitors like Alphabet's (GOOG, GOOGL) YouTube TV service, could help mitigate further subscriber losses:

If cord-cutting is better-than-feared going forward, this could bode well for both Sinclair's future advertising revenue, as well as future revenue from retransmission consent agreements with cable companies.

The Investor Presentation also reiterated other positive news from Sinclair as of late, such as Sinclair's January 2025 entering of a transaction support agreement with creditors. This agreement both extends the maturity of Sinclair's outstanding debt, as well as leaves the company well-positioned to capitalize on likely forthcoming changes to station ownership rules by the FCC.

SBGI Stock Valuation and the Role of De-Regulation

Before diving deeper into the FCC deregulation catalyst, as well as another long-term catalyst for SBGI stock, let's take a look at valuation, to see what the company is worth today, and what it may be worth in the future.

At current prices, Sinclair has a market cap of $909.3 million. Add in $4.28 billion in outstanding debt, and subtract $697 million in cash, and SBGI has an enterprise value of around $4.43 billion. According to Seeking Alpha, SBGI currently trades at a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 9x.

This suggests that EBITDA for 2025 should come in at around $492 million. Compared to peers, Sinclair may appear reasonably priced right now. Gray Media (GTN), for instance, trades at a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 9.3x. E.W. Scripps (SSP), another station owner, trades at a 10.5x EBITDA multiple.

Nexstar, again the largest of the station owners, trades at an even lower EBITDA multiple than these names, with a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 7.3x. Clearly, with GTN, SSP, and even with SBGI, investors are pricing these names as if these companies will be the acquired, not the acquirers, if deregulation leads to another TV station M&A wave.

However, with Sinclair, there is an additional factor to consider when it comes to valuation. Beyond its 193 owned and/or operated TV stations, as well as cable TV network assets like the Tennis Channel, the company also holds a portfolio of non-media investments, through its Sinclair Ventures unit.

According to note 5 of the latest 10-K, Sinclair Ventures investments are on the books at a total valuation of $430 million. Subtracting this figure from Enterprise Value gives us an adjusted EV of $4 billion, which compared to forecasted 2025 EBITDA suggests that Sinclair trades at an adjusted EBITDA multiple of 8.1x.

Irrespective of whether Sinclair is a net buyer or net seller of stations if there's another consolidation wave, subsequent developments could prove highly beneficial for the future valuation of SBGI stock. Let's say Sinclair decides to divest stations, which at one point it considered doing back in 2024.

If "merger mania" in broadcasting renews, even if Sinclair manages to sell stations at an only 9x EBITDA, besides providing the company with significant cash proceeds (for use in de-leveraging the balance sheet), these transactions could also help highlight Sinclair's slight valuation discount to peers, leading to a re-rating.

In the event SBGI's valuation both valued the operating business at 9x EBITDA, and fully took into account Sinclair's investment portfolio, this could push SBGI's gross valuation to $4.86 billion. Subtract debt, and add back cash, and we get a net valuation of around $1.28 billion, or just over $19 per share, around a 37% premium to current prices.

If Sinclair ends up buying more stations, the impact of cost synergies could make the deals highly accretive to investors. For instance, let's say Sinclair were to buy Gray Television or Scripps. Even if Sinclair were to pay up for such companies, cost synergies could more than make up the difference.

Sinclair currently has an EBITDA margin of 22.55%. Gray's EBITDA margin is substantially higher, at 31.27%, but Scripps has one more in line with that of Sinclair, at 23.25%.

If the consolidated enterprise could bring up EBITDA margins to levels akin to that of Nexstar (which has 33.35% EBITDA margins), or even just to the 25%-30% mark, this would represent a tremendous increase in profitability. Taking into account the highly levered nature of SBGI's balance sheet, higher earnings could have an outsized impact on the company's stock price.

Additional Catalysts/Risks to Consider

Station ownership deregulation may be Sinclair's most talked-about catalyst right now, but there's another major catalyst to keep in mind. That would be the potential for Sinclair, like other broadcasters, to monetize its broadcast spectrum.

Recent statements from the FCC suggest that further spectrum auctions are on the agenda. Only time will tell whether this will translate into a second spectrum auction for broadcasters, but if it does, it could produce another windfall for Sinclair.

Better yet, even if it only results in Sinclair receiving spectrum proceeds close to that of the original auction ($313 million), given Sinclair's much smaller market cap today than in the 2010s, such a windfall will likely have a more pronounced impact on SBGI's valuation.

Add the possible per-share value of a spectrum sale (around $4.70 per share) to our re-rating price target, and we have a potential valuation for SBGI nearing $24 per share, or around 71.4% above current prices. Sinclair is also engaged in efforts to monetize spectrum in ways outside outright sales of this asset.

That is, Sinclair has partnered with Scripps, Gray, and Nexstar, to create a joint venture known as EdgeBeam Wireless, a venture that seeks to capitalize on the advent of ATSC 3.0 technology.

In a nutshell, this venture could monetize spectrum, for use cases like streaming video offload, automotive connectivity, and precision navigation. The total annual addressable market for these and other use cases is by EdgeBeam's estimate to be around $7.5 billion.

Yet while deregulation and future spectrum monetization could help push SBGI stock back to the mid-$20s per share (or even higher), there are few major risks to consider. Broadcasting stocks could in theory be more immune to tariffs than, say, a company importing consumer goods.

However, the impact of tariffs/escalating trade tensions on the U.S. economy will undoubtedly have a negative impact on advertising demand. In the quarters ahead, this could be reflected in underwhelming results from Sinclair.

While the recent debt-related developments may put to bed near-term refinancing/debt maturity concerns, poor results in the coming quarters could still weigh heavily on SBGI stock, outweighing the evident positives such as the above-mentioned catalysts.

Bottom Line on SBGI Stock

With far greater levels of leverage than Sinclair, names like GTN and SSP may offer even more substantial levels of potential upside, if the key catalysts for TV broadcasting going forward play out as expected.

Still, rather than owning just one broadcasting stock, and hence leaving one exposed more greatly to company-specific risks, it may be best to purchase a basket of these broadcasting stocks, in order to place a wager on this trend.

I've previously purchased GTN, as well as Entravision Communications (EVC), a name that Seeking Alpha contributor Leland Roach has previously highlighted as a likely beneficiary of aggressive FCC deregulation. I have yet to buy SBGI, but will likely do so, especially if market weakness persists.

Even at current prices, but more so if SBGI pulls back further in the near-term, there is big upside from ongoing regulatory and technological changes in this often-overlooked segment of the media sector.

data.title: Apple: Tariffs Are Another Nail In The Weak Growth Story
data.description:
In the blink of an eye, Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) investors are suddenly on the run. Due to a combination of Chinese tariffs and the failed implementation of AI tools on the iPhone, the stock market is suddenly very concerned about the future in Cupertino. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock due to the sky-high valuation combined with the weak fundamentals that the market is slowly catching onto.

Trade War

Going back years, Apple has faced extra risk due to a manufacturing base in China and escalating trade tensions with the U.S. The tech giant has long benefitted from having manufacturing based in the Asian country, leading to high sales levels from within the country.

The story turned years ago, and Apple sales in China have slumped. For the recent key December quarter, Apple saw iPhone sales plunge 18% in China.

Back in FQ1'22, Apple reported peak quarterly China sales at $25.8 billion. In the latest FQ1, the tech giant saw sales in China slip to only $18.5 billion, down 28% over those 3 years and equivalent levels all the way back in 2015.

The current trade war is adding an extra level of complexity to the Apple investment story. Just this week, President Trump added a 145% tariff on China goods imported into the U.S. leading to an escalation of the trade war.

Apple is possibly the most impacted company in the world, with Evercore ISI estimating 90% of iPhone production still comes from China. Analysts have unanimously estimated iPhones would cost at least $2,000 and upwards of $3,600 to produce in the U.S. making the devise not feasible to manufacture in this country.

Over the weekend, President Trump apparently exempted smartphones and computers from the 145% reciprocal tariffs in a positive development for Apple. Though, the tech giant still faces a 20% tariff on goods imported from China.

Apple generates over 70% of sales from products like the iPhone, iPad and Mac, making the company reliant on cheap overseas manufacturing. Apple has slowly shifted iPhone production to India in a move to reduce reliance on an unreliable trade partner like China, but the country only accounts for upwards of 20% of production now and the goal was to turn a decent amount of these iPhones into local sales, limiting the export levels.

The escalating trade war hits Apple both via higher costs for products produced in China with the large tariffs, along with potential lost sales in the country.

Trade It, Don't Own It

Apple has already fallen from an all-time high of $260 at the end of 2024 to a current price below $200. Now, influential media personality Jim Cramer has suggested now might be the time to sell the stock due to the trade war after years of telling people to "own it, don't trade it" in reference to Apple.

As with most investments, the time to sell becomes obvious in hind set. In fact, the consensus analyst estimates were bullish on the stock heading into the recent market sell-off, with the average price targets sitting at $250.

Now, these price targets are down to $242 and the momentum suggests analysts will continue cutting targets and likely downgrading the stock. A prime example is Morgan Stanley maintaining an Overweight target on the stock while cutting the price target on Apple to $220 from $252, just a month after cutting the target from $275.

Investors now face this constant downbeat prognosis, where previous bulls at much higher prices now suggest the stock is actually worth much less. The problem here is that Apple isn't actually worth the current price, even after this dip.

The stock has consistently traded at a major disconnect with actual numbers. For the last decade, Apple has struggled to even grow outside of the Covid blip where tech demand was pulled forward.

Even before this major trade war, the consensus analyst estimates only forecast roughly 4% revenue growth in FY25. Apple could well struggle to achieve these targets with the world not very happy with the U.S. due to these trade wars, or the tech giant having to eat high tariff costs or pass them on to customers.

The stock still trades at 26x FY25 EPS targets. Somehow, Apple is still more expensive than the other tech giants like Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META). Microsoft (MSFT) trades at a higher PE multiple of nearly 30x, but the enterprise software company is forecast to produce triple the revenue growth at 13% while being heavily invested in the AI race and not directly stuck in the trade war.

Even without Apple at the center of the trade war, the company just doesn't generate the growth rates to warrant the current PE multiple. Apple recently further delayed the rollout of Apple Intelligence features, placing the company far behind in the global AI race.

The stock doesn't warrant any premium valuation. Even a 20x PE multiple just places Apple at the same multiple as Alphabet and Meta with prime AI businesses. The stock would fall to $145 based on the current FY25 EPS target of $7.26, though the tariff wars are likely cut those numbers.

The better method is to value Apple at closer to 2x the consensus EPS growth rate for FY25 of 7.5% for a 15x multiple, placing the stock at closer to $100. With all of the tariff uncertainty, it's even become more likely that Apple doesn't hit this growth rate, reinforcing a recent history of the tech giant struggling to generate any growth.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that despite all of the negatives surrounding Apple, the stock still trades at a massive premium valuation. The stock trades at the highest level in history outside of 2024 and momentum is only starting to shift towards pessimism.

Investors should continue using the elevated stock prices as an opportunity to exit a position, with the recent all-time highs unlikely to be re-tested any time soon. The lower tariffs will help alleviate some disruption fears, but Apple still faces a tougher situation than when the year started.

data.title: MSTY: Selling MSTR Covered Calls Relatively Less Risky In Today's Market
data.description:
Trump's tariffs have created a unique position for Strategy Inc. (MSTR) and particularly its options-selling environment. Unlike many companies, tariffs do not negatively affect MSTR and may even have positive effects for the company as the U.S. dollar falls. Yet, MSTR has still seen relatively higher implied volatility ("IV") levels due to overall elevated market uncertainty. This is good news for the Yieldmax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY). The odds of MSTR losing a significant amount of value within the next few months are lower, and investors can take advantage of volatility from the overall market uncertainty. That said, I wouldn't expect MSTY's distribution to return to previous levels and triple-digit yields. But income investors should still be happy with the income as its most recent $1.34 distribution per share annualizes to a tremendous 80.89%, and an approximate 25% distribution cut would still leave you with 60% yields relative to MSTY's current $19.88 share price. And while Bitcoin spot ETFs, MSTR, and directly holding Bitcoin remain the better alpha play for those bullish on Bitcoin, MSTY can be a strong income enhancer that offers significant cash flow and financial flexibility.

The U.S. Economic Direction and Bitcoin's Potential

MSTR's current IV levels are hovering close to 100, largely due to overall market uncertainty created by Trump's tariffs. Tariffs negatively impact many companies because they rely on global supply chains to produce their goods affordably. Moving manufacturing to the U.S. would likely make production more expensive for many companies, not to mention that moving and reconfiguring your whole supply chain is something that can take a lot of time. This isn't without considering that other countries can and have been fighting back against Trump's tariffs with their own reciprocal tariffs as well as Trump's rapid and unpredictable decisions.

MSTR, however, is unique because its value comes from its large Bitcoin holdings and the financial strategy it uses to add to its Bitcoin reserves. The company is minimally affected by tariffs in that it doesn't rely on any global supply chains. In fact, the company may actually see benefits from tariffs as the U.S. dollar sees its biggest drop since 2022. Much of this stems from a lost of faith by international governments in the U.S. government and a side effect of trimming U.S. dollar reserves is the increase in assets like gold.

As gold goes up, some are betting that Bitcoin will follow along the same path as a "save haven" asset and there are positive notes to support that. In an report from Grayscale, the price of Bitcoin had only fallen moderately on a risk-adjusted level compared to the S&P 500. IV levels for the asset have also hardly changed compared to the market and show that the asset does offer diversification away from the market. If faith in the U.S. government continues to fall and stagflation becomes more of a reality as a result of tariffs, Bitcoin could begin seeing more value as central banks look for alternative holdings in their reserves. Russia and China have reportedly used Bitcoin to settle energy trades and various countries in Europe have also begun looking into Bitcoin as a way to debase themselves from the U.S. dollar.

What This Means For MSTY

For MSTR, the resulting macroeconomic environment is one where the stock is experiencing higher IV levels coming from general market trends that do not actually negatively impact the company. With this consideration, MSTY benefits significantly as Bitcoin's attractiveness in this economic environment makes MSTR unlikely to crash while benefiting from option premiums from overall higher market volatility. That said, I wouldn't expect MSTY's distributions to increase or return to their former levels. If you take a look at MSTY's current call option spreads on MSTR for the next two weeks, the fund has generated about $57M in net credit from its option spreads or about $0.4522 per share. There are another two weeks before MSTY's next distribution but if everything remains more or less the same, a distribution around $1 per share can be expected.

Although MSTY is no longer distributing the triple-digit yields that early MSTY adopters were once used to, there are still very limited places where you can get 60-80% yields on your investment as reliably as MSTY. Of course, the better alpha play if you believe in Bitcoin's continual adoption is to invest directly in Bitcoin, a spot Bitcoin ETF, or MSTR itself. This is because MSTY caps MSTR's upside. The fund does sell call spreads as a measure to allow itself to capture some gains in events of large spikes, but over the long-term, it will lag MSTR.

For income investors, however, MSTY can act as Bitcoin exposure while providing income. While I don't recommend depending on MSTY for your income, a small portion in the fund as a way to diversify from equities and enhance your income can make sense.

MSTY Risks

Of course, MSTY's risks remain high. Though MSTR is relatively immune to the effects of tariffs, it's not immune to Trump's unpredictability. The Trump Administration has so far been positive towards Bitcoin with its establishment of a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve, but it is difficult to assess how Trump may react if Bitcoin takes a larger role in trade. In 1933, President Roosevelt's Executive Order 6102 required U.S. citizens to deliver on or before May 1, 1933, all but a small amount of gold coin, gold bullion, and gold certificates owned by them to the Federal Reserve, in exchange for $20.67 per troy ounce. Although it's hard to imagine that this could happen with Bitcoin, the U.S. government as well as governments across the world does have power to enact similar laws that could negatively impact Bitcoin significantly.

More widespread adoption of Bitcoin is also a double-edged sword for MSTY. Stabilization of Bitcoin will lower MSTR's volatility which is good thing for MSTY in the sense that its fundamental share price stabilize, but bad as the lower volatility levels lead to smaller option premiums. Long-term investors should be prepared for MSTY's distributions to fall and not rely on the fund as their primary income source. With MSTY, you are also relying on the YieldMax fund managers and their strategies to perform well. There are no distribution expectations set so you are dealing with an essentially black-box trading strategy.

Income Enhancer and Portfolio Diversifier

Due to MSTR's unique position in the macroeconomic environment, MSTY can be a great way to diversify your income portfolio with Bitcoin holdings. While I wouldn't depend on MSTY for the majority of your income needs, the fund is worthwhile in small allocations as an income enhancer. Though I wouldn't expect MSTY to generate the same yields that it did a couple of months ago, locking in 60-80% yields at current prices and payouts is still income that few assets can reliably beat. Trump's tariffs have put in MSTY in a spot where it's benefiting from overall market volatility but experiencing minimal negative effects due to MSTR's Bitcoin business which is why I maintain my Buy rating.

data.title: Altria Group: Play Defense If You Expect Uncertain Times
data.description:
I consider Altria Group (NYSE:MO) a strong pick for uncertain times. It's currently my largest holding out of the consumer products portfolio and I'm happy with that as Altria will prove to be resilient during the upcoming market volatility.

It's a "hot topic" all over the global media, so everyone is probably well-aware of what's going on. The U.S. administration with Donald Trump leading its way tries to turn the global trade table around using tariffs. It caused substantial volatility all over the world with S&P over 10% down since its peak in March 2025, despite many shifts during this not even a two week period.

To cut the story short, the trade war between the U.S. and the world turned (at least temporarily) with the both-ways tariffs actually exceeding 100% (US imposed 145% tariffs and China 125%).

Regardless of where it leads from now, Trump's administration proved one thing. It's chaotic. That leads to overall uncertainty, which markets don't like in general - hence the substantial declines we witnessed in April. That's one of the reasons why I decided to prepare a follow-up on Altria to my previous articles and discuss why I consider it a solid pick for such times.

Altria Group is quite resilient to the trade war

What's the President's goal? Apparently, he intends to reindustrialize the U.S. and incentivize businesses to move the production processes back to the country. That can be a problematic process for many industries and even for those with resources and ability to do that, it will take a while.

That's where Altria has the upper hand. Its production facilities are already in the U.S.

PM USA owns and operates a manufacturing facility located in Richmond, Virginia that PM USA uses in the manufacturing of cigarettes (smokeable products segment). PM USA leases portions of this facility to our other subsidiaries for use in the manufacturing of cigars (smokeable products segment) and oral nicotine pouches (oral tobacco products segment).

In addition, PM USA owns a research and technology center in Richmond, Virginia that it leases to ALCS.

The oral tobacco products segment has various manufacturing and processing facilities, the most significant of which are located in Nashville, Tennessee and Hopkinsville, Kentucky.

The plants and properties owned or leased and operated by us are maintained in good condition and are believed to be suitable and adequate for present needs.

On top of that, regardless whether we talk about MO's smoke-free products or traditional cigarettes, its products are simply addictive. Yes, the trade war will likely hurt US-based consumers and thus, MO's main market, but the Company's products won't be a major "cut-back" items on consumers lists due to their addictive nature.

Altria Group is a defensive business

There are several risk factors and headwinds accompanying MO, so let's name them from the get-go. Firstly, its traditional smokeable products keep declining in sales with a substantial, 10% fall recorded in 2024. That's the issue for all tobacco players, but the likes of Philip Morris International (PM) are less exposed to smoke-free trends. PM's markets are less progressive in terms of moving beyond smoking, so its smokeable segment outperforms MO.

Secondly, MO's focus segment of alternative products isn't free of issues. On one hand, the market of oral tobacco and e-vapor users grew to around 28 mln users (soon to be matched with smokers). On the other hand, one of MO's focus markets - e-vapor market, faces severe issues with illicit products, which still constitute around 60% of the market, limiting the growth potential of legitimate businesses.

Regardless of these issues, MO can be considered a defensive business. It's non-cyclical as it operates within the consumer staples industry. On top of that, its products are addictive, which should limit the demand decrease resulting from worse economic environment (low income-related elasticity of the demand suggesting that a given decline in consumers' income should have a far less than proportionate effect on the demand for MO's products).

Moreover, MO upholds a strong profitability of ~50% EBITDA margin on a consolidated level, which constitutes a safety pillow in the case of any potential downturns.

The defensive nature of MO's business is also highlighted by the stock market reactions to the stock vs reactions to the overall market, which can be measured by Beta coefficient. The 5-year Beta for MO amounts to ~0.58 suggesting that MO's stock price reactions are less than proportionate to the S&P 500's changes. During the last 5 years, on average, when the market grew by X percent, MO grew by 0.58 times X. The same applies to declines, meaning that MO is a defensive stock to hold.

Can't hurt to own some MO

All these characteristics come with a cherry on top. Altria Group is quite cheap. Given several risk factors surrounding the business, the market keeps it valuation in check with just a 9.3x EV-to-EBITDA multiple. During my M&A career, I sold numerous far less successful businesses for a much higher multiple. I understand that the market is cautious, but it only suggests that the downside risk of MO is quite limited.

On top of that, MO has a solid shareholder rewarding policy using both attractive quarterly dividends (currently yielding over 7%) and share repurchases:

For the full year, we returned over $10.2 billion of cash to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. We paid $6.8 billion in dividends and our board raised our dividend by 4.1% in August, marking our 59th increase in the last 55 years. We also completed our previously authorized share repurchase program, repurchasing 73.5 million shares for $3.4 billion, representing our largest single-year share repurchase in over two decades.

With all that in mind and assuming that the uncertainty will stick around for the years to come, I consider MO a "buy". Cheers!

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Tariffs For Some
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump carves out exemptions for tariffs. (0:18) Meta vs. the FTC. (2:19) Retail sales will show a last tariff-less consumer picture. (3:30)

The following is an abridged transcript:

President Donald Trump said he will provide an update on the administration's proposed semiconductor tariffs on Monday.

Lat Friday, the Trump administration excluded smartphones, computers and other popular consumer electronics from reciprocal tariffs. The exclusions include computer semiconductors and equipment used to manufacture semiconductors.

The exclusions, published by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency, follow Trump's moves to impose 145% tariffs on products from China and 10% baseline tariffs on almost every other nation.

The 20 categories listed in the notice are exempt from 125% tariffs on Chinese imports and 10% baseline tariffs on other countries. Goods from China will still face a 20% tariff, a levy imposed to force Beijing to crack down on fentanyl.

The exemptions are applicable to popular electronics items such as smartphones, laptops, computers, computer processors, memory chips, flat panel TV displays, solar cells, hard drives.

The guidance should provide some respite to smartphone manufacturers like Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), AI major Nvidia (NVDA), server makers like Super Micro (SMCI) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), computer makers like Dell (DELL) and HP (HPQ), and chip major Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM).

Tech bull Dan Ives, who slashed price targets for Magnificent 7 names like Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA) over Trump's tariffs, called the exclusions "the best news possible for tech investors."

"Big Tech is back off the cliff with these exemptions and this changes the entire situation for tech stocks with this black swan event for the industry removed," he sid. "The U.S. tech industry has a loud voice and despite initial strong pushback against exemptions within the White House the reality of the situation was finally recognized in the Beltway."

On Monday, Meta Platforms (META) will face off against the U.S. Federal Trade Commission in a high-stakes antitrust trial that could lead to Instagram and WhatsApp being divested.

Mark Zuckerberg, is reportedly lobbying Trump and White House officials to agree to a settlement that would prevent his company from facing an antitrust trial.

According to the Wall Street Journal representatives of the social media and tech giant met with Trump and his senior advisers ahead of the trial.

As earnings season ramps up, J&J (JNJ) issues numbers Tuesday.

Wall Street is expecting EPS of $2.59 on revenue of $21.59 billion.

But BofA analysts say JNJ might not be the best tariff read for pure medtech exposure. Goldman Sachs recently upgraded J&J to Buy from Neutral, arguing that concerns surrounding the loss of exclusivity for the company's blockbuster drug Stelara were overdone.

Also on the earnings calendar:

On Monday, Goldman Sachs (GS) and FB Financial (FBK) report.

Joining J&J on Tuesday are Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C) and United Airlines (UAL).

Abbott Labs (ABT) and Kinder Morgan (KMI) weigh in on Wednesday.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), UnitedHealth (UNH), Blackstone (BX) and KeyCorp (KEY) step up Thursday.

On the economic front, March retail sales numbers will give traders the last pre-tariff look at the consumer.

The forecast is for a 1.3% rise in the headline number and a 0.2% rise for the core rate ex-auto.

Wells Fargo economists say: “Consumer confidence extended its string of declines to four months in March as consumers reported a broad deterioration in the assessment of the inflation outlook and overall economic conditions.”

“The collapse in confidence is consistent with stagflationary concerns, which we expect to unfold over the coming year. Big-ticket spending in March and April could see a surge as consumers pull forward those purchases before tariffs take a significant bite. After that, however, we are likely to see a weak consumer spending performance in the second half of the year.”

For income investors, Hormel Foods (HRL) and Universal (UVV) go ex-dividend on Monday. Hormel pays out on May 15 and Universal pays out on May 5.

AbbVie (ABBV), and Abbott Labs (ABT) go ex-dividend Tuesday. They both have a May 15 payout date.

And Colgate-Palmolive (CL) goes ex-dividend on Thursday, paying out on May 15.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs strategist David Kostin highlighted a deterioration in equity market liquidity, saying it has created a negative feedback loop with volatility.

“Sudden liquidity shocks are typically associated with a wider range of returns,” he said.

Goldman’s S&P 500 Liquidity Tracker -- which monitors bid-ask spreads, futures market depth and the price impact of trading volume -- shows that liquidity has sharply declined in the past month, though it remains above pandemic-era lows.

One clear sign of market stress came on April 7, when the bid-ask spread for the median S&P 500 stock widened to 22 basis points. That’s the highest since March 2020. Similarly, the depth of futures markets fell into the 3rd percentile of the past two decades, underscoring the difficulty in executing large trades without moving prices.

Goldman rebalanced its liquidity baskets, which consist of 50 equal-weighted Russell 1000 stocks with the highest and lowest liquidity ratios. High-liquidity names generally offer narrower bid-ask spreads and higher trading volume, while low-liquidity stocks tend to be more thinly traded and volatile.

data.title: MSTY: Big Yield, Big Risk
data.description:
Yieldmax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY) probably stands out as YieldMax's most enticing options-income ETF, with its yield currently quoting 166%. Some may look at such an investment and consider that even getting half that yield should lead to great returns. I feel, however, that there's a mixed bag here, and the bad scenario, should it materialize, would be quite painful, particularly considering the underlying stock with this ETF specifically.

History and Strategy

MSTY hasn't been a bad investment since its inception last year in February. Its total returns have be 173%.

With just over a year of results, MSTY has delivered positive total returns but not quite as high as its underlying stock, Strategy (MSTR). I'll get deeper into why in a bit, but it's worth noting that MSTR pays no dividend, so its returns in this period are entirely a product of price fluctuations.

Looking at MSTY's dividend distributions, which have been paid monthly, even a single distribution would have made for a double-digit yield at an annual rate. Deriving from their options strategy, these dividends have been volatile, heavily influenced by the movement of MSTR's own price. We see that dividends rose last autumn as MSTR's price did.

Removing the dividend factor, MSTY's price returns have been negative, at -15.42%. This owes to the nature of YieldMax's synthetic covered call strategy.

Instead of buying shares of MSTR outright and writing calls on those, YieldMax takes a different approach. They sell MSTR put contracts on cash or a short-term position (such as Treasury Bills) and use the premiums earned to purchase MSTR call contracts at the same strike price. This synthesizes a long-term position for the period of the contracts.

They they write call contracts at a higher strike price than the paired call and put position. This premium, plus the interest from their cash/short-term position is the basis for the fund's income.

Risks To MSTY

We've seen what goes well when MSTR has the good kind of volatility, where the price goes up quickly. Options contracts can command large premiums, and the put contracts can expire without being assigned to MSTY.

Yet, if one looks at the charts I shared, the gains in MSTR are not normally felt as strongly in MSTY, while the drops are. This owes to the nature of the fund's strategy. If MSTR crashes, the fund would get assigned the puts or forced to close them out at a loss. While this is an actively managed fund that should allow for flexibility, MSTY's regular presence in a covered call position caps their upside in the event of a rebound. It's a fund that has a much easier time riding MSTR's way down than up.

What I say is true of all of these YieldMax funds, though, but there are specifics that can cause this risk to materialize for MSTY, and I'll refer to my own coverage of MSTR.

One difference between MSTR and most other stocks behind the YieldMax funds is that it doesn't have much of a business behind it (hence no dividends from earnings). The slide above discusses the growth of MSTR's Bitcoin (BTC-USD) portfolio. While Strategy has a software business, this is not the main focus of the business anymore, and most investor materials nowadays discuss management's plans for investing in BTC.

One thing I found perplexing about MSTR is that it even trades at a major premium to the value of its BTC holdings, often around twice as much. The best explanation for this is provided by the slide above. It doesn't attempt to explain why MSTR is priced as it is. Rather, it shows the financing strategy of the company, wherein they sell shares and convertible securities in order to purchase BTC. Long-term, they believe they can produce "BTC Yield" by diluting the common at a slower rate than the growth in BTC's value.

Seen above, this makes MSTR more volatile, declining more sharply than BTC, as such declines create the risk that MSTR is diluting to purchase a poor asset. Over time, this would affect MSTY, but the short-term dips can spoil MSTY's put positions as described earlier, with little room to bounce back due to the covered calls.

In essence, the risk is that, unlike most other income investments, there's no secure principal. In fact, the nature of the income makes the principal very unsecure.

Conclusion

While the yield is there if things go well, the case that MSTY should be a Buy is a difficult one to make. It employs a derivative strategy on MSTR, which itself functions like a derivative on BTC with its own similarly speculative strategy. In my conclusion on MSTR, I wrote:

I take issue with the idea that selling shares at a premium to the portfolio to buy more creates "BTC yield," any more than Berkshire doing the same would create "KO yield" with more shares of Coca-Cola. Without a source of earnings (something Berkshire has) to support this BTC accumulation, it's not clear why those who believe in BTC would aim for MSTR instead of buying the BTC directly.

I think a similar observation could be made among the these synthetic covered call funds. If someone believes in the basic idea at work with this fund, there is there is Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF (YBTC), which employs the same type of strategy but directly on BTC, without the risks that come with MSTR.

Because there is a mixture of upside and downside, as well as credible alternatives (one could also just buy BTC if they insist on exposure to crypto), I think Hold makes much more sense than Buy for this ETF.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Banking Environment Becoming More Challenging
data.description:
The report by JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) of an excellent first quarter was overshadowed by macro events that promise uncertainty for some time to come. Banks hate uncertainty because it threatens the matching function that's so important to banking. Bank stocks (including this one in my opinion) are unlikely to do well until that uncertainty goes elsewhere.

We recently saw a reversal of the tariff strategy in a material way almost as fast as it was enacted. That strategy change was likely caused (in my opinion) by the bond market because the bond market has a large effect on banking. Interest rates began to rise and there was a Treasury auction (among other things) to get through. The issue of deleveraging (which in my opinion showed itself enough to cause the tariff reversal) can be devastating on so many levels, from the government to the banks.

Jamie Dimon, CEO noted the effects of all of this in his first quarter report.

The economy is facing considerable turbulence (including geopolitics), with the potential positives of tax reform and deregulation and the potential negatives of tariffs and “trade wars,” ongoing sticky inflation, high fiscal deficits and still rather high asset prices and volatility. As always, we hope for the best but prepare the Firm for a wide range of scenarios.

That being said, we continue to believe it is prudent to maintain excess capital and ample liquidity in this environment – our CET1 ratio remained very strong at 15.4%, and we have an extraordinary amount of liquidity, with $1.5 trillion of cash and marketable securities.

This quote is from the first quarter earnings press release. Being profitable enough to maintain the kind of liquidity that this bank is talking about is a feat all by itself. There are many competitors that really do not have the option noted above.

Furthermore, a bank like JPMorgan Chase has a fair amount of income that exists besides strictly banking to help make it through any banking downturn. But much of what's going on threatens all the business lines of the bank. This is something that Mr. Dimon has emphasized for some time in a press conference as well as the first quarter conference call.

That interest rate rise was (in my opinion) a wakeup call for the banking industry as is the mention that customers are "becoming conservative" or "are pulling back". Turmoil and uncertainty threaten matching function in that customers who thought they had money to invest long term may suddenly change their mind because they need liquidity. This causes the liability side of the matching to begin to decline before the bank had projected.

This bank has excess liquidity so that unraveling matches can be protected. There's further money coming in from related functions like mortgage sales and loan servicing as well as investment banking. All of this can be combined with less current matching so that a money center bank can get through the current situation with only a few "scratches and dents." But there will be banks that will go under if the turmoil and uncertainty does not come to an end fast because business is putting conservative plans into play that will bring on a recession at least. This does not include what the political situation will cause, which currently has many people alarmed.

Summary

The political situation has managed to override what's expected to be excellent banking results. The reason for this is that there's an attempt to fix the "wrongs" bluntly and really without communicating ahead of time the plan. The result is a lot of uncertainty, fears and rumors. None of this is particularly good for banking.

As a result, the stock price action is likely to move based upon the political situation rather than based on results because the market fears what lies ahead. The market also fears that banking has "a target on its back." The sooner this turmoil heads elsewhere, the better it will be for banking.

First Quarter

First quarter results were excellent. If there was ever a place to invest in the current situation, this is likely to be the place when it comes to banking.

Given the fantastic history of the bank, results like this would normally reinforce a strong buy consideration. However, the volatile political situation can quickly unravel even the best plans. Therefore, investors need to decide if they want to hang on for what's going to be a very rough ride (possibly for four years).

The whole industry is very afraid of the "deleveraging threat" because it showed itself for a brief moment during this past week. Banking does not ever want that threat, even on the horizon.
